meta.description: Defines the 7 session types used in the Tarbiyyah Audit. Each session type has specific context fields, a scan-for prompt list, and a mapping to relevant framework indicators. Indicators marked 'primary' are the main focus; 'secondary' are also assessable. Indicators NOT in either list should be scored N/A for that session type.

classroom.name: Classroom Observation
classroom.description: Observation of one teacher in one lesson, ~50 minutes. The most frequent session type during the audit.
scanFor: How the lesson opened (basmala, du'a, niyyah, presence vs rushed)
Tawheed/Islamic connection made to the subject content
Prophetic examples or Seerah references used during the lesson
How a correction or disruption was handled (public vs private, tone)
Peer dynamics - do students treat each other with ukhuwwah or not?
Physical environment - walls, displays, Islamic presence, currency of displays
Questions asked - comprehension only, or reflective and identity-engaging?
Student voice - are students thinking and contributing, or just answering?
Teacher's emotional regulation under pressure (any disruption, how it was met)
Moments where the teacher noticed a struggling or quieter student
How the lesson closed (Hamdala, du'a, reflection, or just bell)
Use of student names (positive references vs only corrective)
Any moment where a student raised a genuine faith question and how it was met
Differentiation - does the teacher adapt to individual students?
5D Thinking application - is it visible, planned, integrated, or absent?
Islamic intellectual tradition referenced (especially in non-Islamic subjects)
Connection between subject content and the student's iman or identity
Stories and narrative as teaching tools (Seerah, Quranic stories, lived examples)

teacher_meeting.name: Teacher 1-on-1 Meeting
teacher_meeting.description: Individual conversation with a teacher to understand their formation journey, role identity, and view of the school.
scanFor: Can they articulate the school's tarbiyyah vision in their own words?
Do they describe their role as murabbi or just subject teacher?
How do they connect 5D, REAL Schools, Murabbi Project, and Islamic Studies?
What's their own Islamic formation journey - is it supported by the school?
How do they handle faith questions from students?
Do they feel overwhelmed by change management fatigue?
How do they communicate with parents - deficit-focused or formation-focused?
Can they describe specific students' formation journeys?
Islamic rationale for behaviour framework - do they have one?
What Islamic content lives in their general subject (if not Islamic Studies)?
Do they describe leadership as modelling Islamic character?
What gets in the way of them being a murabbi - capacity, training, time?
How do they describe the coherence of school programmes?
Where do they go for scholarly guidance when they have a question?

teacher_focus_group.name: Teacher Focus Group
teacher_focus_group.description: Group discussion with 4-6 teachers, typically across departments. Surfaces collective culture, programme coherence, and shared (or unshared) language.
scanFor: Shared language across departments, or lack of it
Do different departments talk to each other about formation?
Collective sense of purpose vs siloed execution
How do staff describe leadership - modelling Islamic character?
Fatigue, workload, capacity for new initiatives
Which programmes feel coherent, which feel fragmented?
Who do they see as the scholarly anchor in the school?
How do they describe the Murabbi Project's actual reach?
Do they disagree with each other in healthy ways (shura culture)?
Any tension they're willing to name about the tarbiyyah agenda?
Is there a common understanding of what a graduate should be?
Do they speak about students' formation collectively, or only academically?
How do they describe their own Islamic formation as staff?
Where are the gaps in cross-departmental tarbiyyah collaboration?

student_focus_group.name: Student Focus Group
student_focus_group.description: Group discussion with 6-8 students, typically same year group. The only place to gather direct evidence on graduate outcomes, identity, and love of deen.

contextFields.label: How were students selected? (random, recommended, mixed)

scanFor: Can students articulate the school's Islamic vision?
Do they name specific teachers as murabbis? Who?
Do they feel known by adults beyond their grades?
Can they describe Islamic content in non-Islamic Studies subjects?
How do they describe salah at school - meaningful or procedural?
Is Adab class valuable to them or filler?
Do they ask faith questions openly, or mask doubts?
What's their relationship with the Quran - recitation only or tadabbur?
Identity - confident Muslim, or torn between identities?
Love of deen - genuine, or performance for school?
What do they do Islamically that the school didn't teach them?
Peer culture - is ukhuwwah present, or just general decency?
Who do they go to with a personal or faith struggle?
Can they describe how their academic learning connects to their iman?
Do they have a sense of who they want to be as a Muslim graduate?
How do they describe their teachers' Islamic character?
What would they change about the Islamic dimension of their school?

leadership_meeting.name: Leadership Meeting
leadership_meeting.description: Conversation with senior leadership (principal, deputies, head of tarbiyyah). Primary source of evidence for vision, strategic priority, and programme coherence.
scanFor: How is the tarbiyyah vision articulated, and does everyone agree?
Is tarbiyyah resourced (budget, time, accountability)?
How are trade-offs made when tarbiyyah competes with academic priorities?
Who is accountable for tarbiyyah outcomes specifically?
How do they describe the coherence of their programmes?
What's the plan for scholarly guidance?
How do they describe their relationship with staff - shura or top-down?
Graduate profile - is it defined, written, operational?
Minimum Nisab - how developed is their thinking?
What signals genuine commitment vs aspirational language?
Concrete decisions vs general aspirations - what have they actually changed?
Are there contradictions between what they say and what they describe doing?
What's their succession plan - if leadership changed, would tarbiyyah survive?
How do they understand the relationship between Islamic and academic excellence?

environment_walk.name: Environment Walk
environment_walk.description: Walk through the school's physical and cultural spaces - corridors, masjid, lunch areas, transitions. Observe what's felt, heard, seen.
scanFor: Visual Islamic presence in corridors - meaningful or decorative?
Are displays current, student-generated, connected to learning?
Salah - how is it conducted, atmosphere, adult presence?
Transitions between classes - dhikr, adab, rhythm?
Informal time - student behaviour when unsupervised
Peer dynamics - visible inclusion or hierarchy?
Staff presence in informal spaces - relational or supervisory?
Language overheard - Islamic vocabulary natural or absent?
Masjid/prayer space - treated as sacred or functional?
Student self-regulation around Islamic norms without teachers
Gender dynamics in shared spaces
How does the school feel different from a non-Islamic school in informal settings?
Wayfinding and signage - Islamic framing or generic?
What's the first impression upon entering?

document_review.name: Document Review
document_review.description: Review of curriculum documents, schemes of work, lesson plans, policies, parent communications.

contextFields.label: Document type (scheme of work, lesson plan, policy, etc.)

scanFor: Coherence and sequencing across year groups
Formation intent built into lesson plans vs content-only
Tadabbur presence in Quran programme docs
Applied Islam content - designed or improvised?
Adab scope and sequence - real progression?
5D integration visible in general subject schemes of work
Pastoral/murabbi hooks in lesson design
Assessment criteria - Islamic framing or purely academic?
Graduate profile referenced in curriculum design?
Evidence of review, iteration, improvement over time
Reflective and experiential components built into the curriculum
Use of Prophetic stories and Islamic narrative as teaching tools
Differentiation for formation needs, not just academic
Quality of writing, professionalism, fitness-for-purpose
Date of last update - is the document live or dormant?

sessionStatuses.draft: Session created but no notes yet
sessionStatuses.notes: Notes captured, debrief not yet run
sessionStatuses.debrief: Debrief questions generated, awaiting auditor answers
